critique.summary: The article describes the author's process of reverse-engineering Viktor, an AI coworker developed by Zeta Labs, by extracting and analyzing its internal files and logs. The author then used this information to recreate the system and released an open-source version called OpenViktor.
mainPoints: Zeta Labs announced Viktor, an AI coworker connecting to over 3,000 tools and quickly gaining popularity.
The author obtained a backup of Viktor's workspace, including SDK code, logs, and skill prompts.
Analysis revealed detailed internal architecture, workflows, API schemas, and error handling strategies.
Using the gathered information, the author recreated Viktor's system and released OpenViktor as an open-source project.
OpenViktor can be self-hosted or accessed via a managed instance with usage limits.
critique.truthfulness: The article's claims about reverse-engineering Viktor are consistent with the detailed technical descriptions and file structures presented. The author provides concrete examples such as file names, log entries, and workflow descriptions that support the narrative. However, the article does not cite external sources or verification from Zeta Labs or independent parties to confirm the accuracy of the reverse-engineering or the completeness of the recreated system. The claim of reaching 1M ARR in 3 hours is attributed to Zeta Labs but not independently verified. Overall, the technical details appear plausible but lack corroboration from multiple sources.
considerations: The ethical and legal implications of reverse-engineering proprietary AI software are not discussed.
Potential security or privacy risks related to accessing logs and internal data of Viktor are not explored.
The article does not provide perspectives from Zeta Labs or other stakeholders about the reverse-engineering.
The stability, performance, and feature parity of OpenViktor compared to the original Viktor are not evaluated.
No independent verification or peer review of the recreated system is presented.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around the legality and ethics of reverse-engineering Viktor's software, with some commenters highlighting the simplicity of the product and others emphasizing the potential for competition and innovation. There are also concerns about intellectual property rights and the ethical motivations behind the reverse-engineering effort.

comments.author: redfloatplane
comments.text:
There are gonna be some really interesting legal decisions to read in the coming years, that’s for sure…

---

The rest of this comment is irrelevant, but leaving for posterity, I had the wrong Viktor - it's getviktor.com not viktor.ai:

Edit: this one particularly interesting to me as both parties are in the EU. VIKTOR.ai is a Dutch company and the author of this post is Polish.

The ToS for Viktor.ai include the following fun passages:

> 18.1. The Agreement and these Terms & Conditions are governed by Dutch law and the Agreement and these Terms & Conditions will be interpreted in accordance with Dutch law.

18.2. All disputes arising from or arising in connection with the Agreement and/or the Terms & Conditions will be submitted exclusively to the competent court in Rotterdam, The Netherlands.

7.3. The Customer is not permitted to change, remove or make unrecognizable any mark showing VIKTOR's Intellectual Property Rights to the Software. The Customer is not permitted to use or register any trademark or design or any domain name of VIKTOR or a similar name or sign in any country.

8.5. The Customer may not cause or allow any reproduction, imitation, duplication, copying, sale, resale, leasing or trading of the Services and/or the Software, or any part thereof.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a detailed and accurate summary of the legal framework governing Viktor.ai, citing specific terms of service that are relevant to the discussion.

comments.author: yashasolutions
comments.text:
Nice. Probably worth making a local copy before it gets taken down.

(Re: legal - why even bother with a court decision when it’s on GitHub? A takedown is much simpler. We've seen this before, like when Meta went after people reverse-engineering their API)

That said there may not be much here thats actually protectable. It's mostly a CLI orchestrating other tools, and the same functionality could likely be reproduced fairly easily, especially with AI.

Still, props to him for writing a proper blog post and explaining the process
comments.analysis: The comment correctly assesses the technical nature of the software and the practical implications of legal actions like takedowns, providing a balanced view.

comments.author: hmokiguess
comments.text:
A lot of folks talking about the legality basis of this approach, I think the focus should be more around the true moat behind these hype growth hack companies out there.

This sort of exposure is good, in my opinion it enables competition to emerge with true business moat rather than just "do it first, do it fast, worry last" type of thing we're seeing.
comments.analysis: This comment shifts the discussion towards business strategy and competition, offering a broader perspective that is relevant and insightful.

comments.author: empath75
comments.text:
I just want to point out _how easy it is_ to build any of these tools yourself.

I got inspired by nano-claw and built on some of it's ideas to build a whole k8s hosted autonomous agent platform and got it into production in 2 weeks. It's just some api calls and container orchestration. The only hard problem _and it is hard_, is securing it, because you basically have to treat the agents as potentially malicious.
comments.analysis: The comment provides a practical viewpoint on the technical challenges involved, emphasizing security as the main difficulty, which is a valid and useful insight.

comments.author: kklisura
comments.text: I find it fascinating that this is just some _plumbing_ around some LLM - sold as a product and "AI coworker". They don't own the LLM model. You're just one silent update from not delivering what you promised.
comments.analysis: This comment accurately points out that the product relies on existing LLM technology, highlighting potential risks in the product's value proposition.

comments.author: klustregrif
comments.text: Ignorering the questionable legality of this entire thing, which might fall out in favor of it being a legal activity. Calling the project OpenViktor is as dumb as committing secrets to GitHub. For the love of reason call it something else.
comments.analysis: The comment expresses a strong opinion on naming and legality, which is valid but somewhat subjective and less detailed than others.

comments.author: dirk94018
comments.text: "Driven by curiosity and envy, I tested it out." Two days of work driven by envy. Not what open source should be about.
comments.analysis: This comment is more of a moral judgment on the motivation behind the project, lacking constructive or factual support.

comments.author: popcorncowboy
comments.text: Every part of this is genuinely funny. Viktor launching basically OpenClaw for slack (because claw is MIT so yolo let's go mac some monnney and PJ outta Dubai etc etc). Some guy getting all their source code just by asking Viktor for it. Then rebuilding their core in a couple days because there's basically nothing there. push MIT to Github. TeamViktor's faces surely? But they're busy diamond handsing all the way to the moon so probably don't even care. XD
comments.analysis: This comment is sarcastic and mocking, providing little substantive analysis or constructive input.

comments.author: cubefox
comments.analysis: A very brief and subjective statement without explanation or evidence, offering minimal value to the discussion.

comments.author: petterroea
comments.text: Is this reverse-engineering or "just" data exfiltration
comments.analysis: Raises a relevant question about the nature of the activity, but lacks further elaboration or context.

comments.author: vixalien
comments.text: How did you make the image at https://github.com/zggf-zggf/openviktor/blob/main/docs/asset...?
comments.analysis: This comment is a simple question unrelated to the main discussion, contributing little to the analysis.

comments.author: hexnuts
comments.text: @zggf I'm developing something of a similar concept. I wanted to know if I could chat with you for a few minutes.
comments.analysis: A direct request for communication that does not add analytical value to the discussion.

comments.author: hirako2000
comments.analysis: A playful and non-substantive comment that does not contribute meaningful insight.
